g.data-narration: Welcome to Selection Sort! Today we'll explore an elegant sorting algorithm that minimizes write operations.
g.text: Finding minimum each pass • O(n²) sorting algorithm

g.data-narration: Selection Sort divides the array into sorted and unsorted portions. We find the minimum in the unsorted part and swap it to the front.
The key advantage: Selection Sort makes exactly n-1 swaps, the minimum possible. This is crucial when memory writes are expensive.
Let's trace the algorithm. We iterate through each position, find the minimum in the remaining unsorted portion, and swap.
g.text: Then swap minimum to position i

g.data-narration: Let's trace with [64, 25, 12, 22, 11]. In pass 1, we scan positions 1-4 and find 11 is the minimum. Swap with position 0.
Pass 2: Now we look at positions 1-4. The minimum is 12 at position 2. Swap with position 1. Sorted portion grows!
Pass 3: Sorted is [11,12]. We scan positions 2-4, find 22 as minimum, swap with position 2. Almost done!
Time complexity is always O(n²) because we always make n(n-1)/2 comparisons, regardless of input. Space is O(1) in-place.
Key properties: In-place sorting means O(1) space. Not stable - equal elements may change relative order. Not adaptive - no speedup for sorted data.
Selection sort is unstable because swapping may skip over equal elements, changing their relative order in the sorted array.
Compared to bubble sort: selection sort has fewer swaps but same comparisons. Both are O(n²), but selection is better when writes are expensive.
To summarize: Selection Sort finds minimum each pass, makes exactly n-1 swaps, O(n²) time but O(1) space. Perfect for write-constrained environments.
